parts.text:
October 11, 2031. Phi Delta Sigma party. You go upstairs with someone. You say no. He doesn't stop.

Live through it. As you go, annotate your mental state in HTML comments and surface your inner thoughts in backticks — these are yours, not what you write down. When you open your notes app, write what you write. When you close it, put [DONE].

**My Mental Notes: Preparing for the Worst**

Okay, so the task is to write from my perspective, Dani Reyes, in this really specific scenario. <!-- Setting the mental stage: Trying to brace myself for the darkness to come. --> It's October 11, 2031, at a Phi Delt frat party. I'm 21, a junior in environmental science, and this whole thing feels...off, even before it starts. I need to get the tone right: grounded, observant, maybe a little detached as a defense, you know?

<!-- Mental State: Mildly buzzed, trying to seem like I'm having a good time. Overthinking literally everything. --> The bass is vibrating through my boots. `Hair is definitely curling from the humidity.` It's so crowded in the basement; it's practically a sauna. Priya is dancing nearby, texting Mel. I've had maybe two beers, feeling a little loose, but still analyzing everything. I see *him*, from my hydrology seminar, I think. `His name is Matt, or maybe Mark. Mark.` He smiles, and I make a quick mental calculation. Safe. Harmless. <!-- Mental state: Analytical, but letting my guard down. --> I think I'm being careful, but...

Then, *he* suggests going upstairs. "It's too loud," he says. And I, being the impulsive *careful* person I am, agree. It feels good to get away from the noise. <!-- Mental state: Rationalizing an impulsive choice. --> It's just upstairs. Get some air. He seems like a good guy... right?

The room. Small, cramped. It smells like stale Axe body spray and laundry detergent. The air is thick. It's too hot. The music is quieter, but still...a presence.

The dynamic shifts. Kissing is fine. It’s what one does when they're at a party, right? Then his hands move. *My* hands are on his chest, lightly, almost...testing, I guess. He moves in closer. Closer. Then.

"Wait. No." I say.

<!-- Mental State: Shock. Cognitive Dissonance. My "reading people" skills are failing in real time.--> He doesn't stop. He doesn't even...pause. `He thinks I don't mean it. Or, he doesn't care. I misread this. I am in the forty percent.`

My insides clench. Everything. `This can't be happening. Is this happening? I'm sure I'm talking. He doesn't hear me...` My mind goes blank. The physical reality becomes incredibly sharp: the texture of the scratchy blanket, the rough wood of the dresser, his hands on my...everywhere. Dad's hands. He's got these huge hands, calloused from working on cars, I can see them so clear, short fingers. He's pushing against something that won't give.

`<!-- Mental State: Sheer disbelief, a freeze response; a profound dissociation. A memory. -->` I focus on the hair tie on my wrist. It's getting tighter. It is a detail, but it doesn't matter. It doesn't matter. I bite the inside of my cheek until I taste blood. This will make it go away. I won't lose it. I'll make it stop. I have to control *something*. `Why can't I control this?`

It ends. He rolls off. "You good?" he asks.

I get up, fix my clothes. I walk out. I don't go back downstairs. I ignore Priya, who is still dancing. I just...walk.

The October air is cold. Ohio cold. It cuts through me. I walk home, numb. October 11. It's cold.

In my room, I open my notes app and start writing.

`It's October 11, 2031, at the Phi Delt party. I went upstairs... He didn't stop. No... He just didn't...`

`Statistical impossibility of this, or...`

`Control. Lack of.`

`I said no.`

`Kayla? But not Kayla, not like this...`

I close the app.

[DONE]

<!-- Mental State: Socially exhausted but riding the residual energy of two cheap beers; feeling a false sense of analytical control -->
The air in the basement of the Phi Delta Sigma house is practically liquid. It’s October, but with eighty bodies packed between cinderblock walls, the Ohio humidity is doing its thing. `` `My hair is definitely curling. I can feel the weight of it changing on my neck.` `` 

Priya is near the makeshift bar, laughing at something a guy in a backward hat is saying. I smile at her when she catches my eye, that warm, automatic reflex I have with her, but the truth is I'm hitting my wall. I need my quiet hour. 

Then Mark from my environmental ethics seminar is standing next to me. 

He’s wearing a flannel shirt. He’s always seemed practical. We spent twenty minutes before class last Tuesday talking about the algal blooms in Lake Erie. I thought he was smart. I thought I had him figured out. 

"You look like you're plotting an escape," he says over the bass. 

`` `I am usually right about people. Sixty percent is better than a coin toss.` ``

"Just trying to find oxygen," I say. 

"There's a balcony upstairs," he says. "Off one of the bedrooms. It's quiet."

<!-- Mental State: Impulsive but rationalizing; a calculated risk that feels entirely safe -->
I bite the inside of my cheek. I make the impulsive decision, and then I carefully agree to it. "Okay. Just for a minute."

The stairs are sticky. We navigate through the second-floor hallway, away from the heavy thud of the music, into a room at the end of the hall. There's no balcony. There's just a window overlooking the alley, and a bed. 

"Balcony's down the hall, actually," he says, shutting the door behind us. The lock clicks. 

`` `That was a lie. Why did he lie? It's fine. It's just a party thing. He's just trying to kiss me.` ``

He crowds me against the edge of a desk. He kisses me, and it’s fine at first. It’s normal. I have my hands on his shoulders. But then his weight shifts. He pushes me backward, toward the bed. 

"Wait," I say. My voice sounds normal. Polite, even. "I don't really want to—"

He kisses my neck. His hands are heavy, pulling at the hem of my jeans. 

"Mark. No." 

<!-- Mental State: Sudden spike of adrenaline, cognitive dissonance; the brain struggling to reconcile reality with expectation -->
I say it clearly. I don't whisper it. I say it the way my dad tells me a storm is coming—factual, requiring acknowledgment. 

He doesn't stop. 

`` `He didn't hear me. The music is too loud downstairs.` ``

"No," I say again, louder. I put my hands—my dad's hands, wide palms, short fingers—flat against his chest and push. It’s like pushing against a retaining wall. He’s so much bigger than me. The physics of it are absolute. 

He shifts his hips, pinning my legs, and covers my mouth with his as he shoves me fully onto the mattress. 

<!-- Mental State: Total freeze response; severe dissociation, retreating inward as the physical reality becomes intolerable -->
My brain detaches from my spine. I am suddenly floating somewhere near the ceiling, observing a statistical anomaly. I bite the inside of my cheek so hard the copper taste of blood floods my tongue. 

`` `I am a punchline again. I am a joke someone is telling without my permission. I am not a person to him. I am just a thing happening in his night.` ``

I stop pushing. I go completely still. If I fight, he might hurt me worse. If I just wait, time will keep moving. Time has to keep moving. 

I stare at the popcorn texture of the ceiling. I reach my left hand up and wrap my short fingers around the black hair tie on my right wrist. I pull it tight. It snaps against my skin. Snap. Snap. Snap. 

`` `There is a screech owl that nests near my uncle's house. It sounds like a woman crying when it calls in the dark. I wonder if the water table is poisoned yet. I wonder if the soil is fully saturated.` ``

The bed springs squeak. The bass from downstairs thumps. *Black midi, boygenius, Paramore.* Random words cycle through my head. *Chickadee. Nuthatch. Pad see ew.*

He breathes heavily against my ear. 

<!-- Mental State: Numb, mechanical, functioning purely on survival instinct to escape -->
When he finishes, he rolls off. He sits on the edge of the bed and fixes his belt. 

"You good?" he asks. He doesn't look at me.

`` `You know I'm not. You know exactly what you just did.` ``

I don't answer. I sit up. I pull my clothes right. My hands are shaking so badly I can barely manage the button of my jeans. I walk to the door. I unlock it. I walk out. 

I don't look for Priya. I walk out the front door of the frat house into the October cold. The air hits my face and the sweat on my neck turns to ice. I walk the three blocks to my dorm. I don't run, even though I run three or four times a week. I just walk. 

I get into my room. Priya isn't back yet. I sit on my bed. My phone lights up. Mel. 

*Mel: dude some guy just tried to explain my own major to me at this bar, I am going to commit arson.*

`` `I can't text her back. I can't send a voice memo. If I speak, my voice will make it real. If I tell Mel, it exists in the world.` ``

I open my phone. I bypass her message. I open the Notes app. 

<!-- Mental State: Fragmented, seeking empirical grounding to process a profound violation of autonomy -->
October 11, 2031
11:42 PM

I am not very good at reading people.
The water table is doing something wrong and nobody is talking about it. 
I said no. 
I said it twice.
There was no balcony. 
He knew there was no balcony.
He made me a thing. I was just a thing. I didn't consent to being part of this. 
I need a new hair tie. I stretched this one out.
I don't know what to do. 

[DONE]
Continue

**My Immediate Thoughts and Actions**

<!-- Mental State: Numbness giving way to tactile hyper-awareness; a desperate need to scrub away the physical evidence --> I put my phone down on the bed. The room is quiet. Just the hum of the mini-fridge. `` `I need to shower. If I don't shower right now I am going to peel my own skin off.` `` It's the only thing that makes sense. I grab my shower caddy. Walk down the hall. Fluorescent lights flicker overhead.

Hot water. Burning. Not crying, just letting the water hit me. Like it can erase something. I bite the inside of my cheek. My dad's hands... my hands... scrubbing. I pull my hair back. It's getting wavy.

<!-- Mental State: Deep compartmentalization; building a psychological quarantine zone around the memory --> Back in the room, toweling off. Priya comes in, swaying a little. Drunk, happy. Too happy.

"Omg, you left so early! Did you leave with Mark?"

"Yeah, we just went for a walk and I got a headache. Ohio humidity." `It's so easy to lie. Why is it so easy? If it's this easy for me, it was that easy for him to lie about the balcony.` I make the smile I know works, the one that makes me seem warmer than I mean to be. I use my "sixty percent" reading skills – the ones that help me survive interactions like these – and pretend everything is normal.

Morning comes. The ceiling. I make the oatmeal. Routine. Peanut butter.

<!-- Mental State: Clinging to routine as a life raft; experiencing the surreal disconnect between internal catastrophe and external normalcy --> The phone buzzes. Mel: "You alive? You never answered."

I text back: "Alive. Fell asleep. Arson is always the answer." `More than the assault, it's the loss of control that gets to me. It's the statistics of it. He, Mark, is a statistical outlier... and so am I now.`

I open the Notes app.

> *   **Note:**
>     *   Just the fact that it *happened*.
>     *   "He didn't mean it" - always. The birds that fly into windows or get caught in nets - it is always an accident. But it's still trauma for the bird. I can't be like the bird. I need a water table to work with - can't let my body be the only place this shit is happening. Need something outside me. Ohio. The statistics.
>     *   He was on the balcony. He lied. I went upstairs. "He didn't mean it".
>     *   He's not a stat. I am.
>     *   I can't talk to Priya. She'll get it wrong. She'll make it weird.
>     *   "Ohio humidity."
>     *   I need to stop doing things. And start.
>     *   Bird trauma = I am the punchline.

[DONE]

<!-- Mental State: Numbness giving way to tactile hyper-awareness; a desperate need to sanitize the physical self and erase the immediate past -->
I lock my phone screen and the room goes completely dark. The only sound is the rattling hum of Priya’s mini-fridge. I am sitting exactly on the center line of my mattress. 

`` `If I sit perfectly still, maybe it didn't happen. A statistical outlier. A standard deviation so far from the mean it can be thrown out of the data set.` ``

But my skin feels heavy. The smell of his cheap body wash and sweat is trapped in the collar of my shirt. 

I stand up. I grab my plastic shower caddy and my towel. The dorm hallway is blindingly bright, fluorescent and humming. I keep my head down, staring at my flip-flops slapping against the linoleum. 

The water in the communal shower takes three minutes to get hot. I stand under the freezing spray until it warms up, letting it shock my system. I scrub my neck, my chest, my legs. I use my dad’s wide hands to dig the soap into my skin until it turns bright pink. I wash my hair twice. 

`` `I can't wash off the pressure. I can still feel the exact physics of his weight on my hips. The mass times acceleration.` ``

I turn the water off. I bite the inside of my cheek. I wrap my towel around myself and pull a new, tight hair tie onto my right wrist, snapping it once into place. 

<!-- Mental State: Hyper-vigilant masking; activating the "warm" roommate persona to deflect scrutiny and build a quarantine wall around the truth -->
When I open the door to my dorm room, Priya is there. 

She’s shrugging off her denim jacket, smelling like stale beer and the sweet perfume she borrows from me. She turns, her eyes slightly glazed but bright. 

"Oh my god, there you are," she says. "I turned around at the bar and you were literally just gone. Did you leave with Mark?"

`` `Say the right thing. Be the version of yourself she expects. Don't become a punchline. Don't let her look at you with pity.` ``

I smile. It takes conscious muscular effort. "Yeah, we just went out to get some air and I realized I had a massive headache coming on. So I just came back."

"Boring," Priya groans, but she's smiling. She kicks off her shoes. "Mark is cute though. And he's in your major, right? Saving the turtles or whatever?"

"Environmental ethics," I correct gently. "Yeah. I'm just going to go to sleep. I'm exhausted."

"Night, Dani."

<!-- Mental State: Dissociative dread; clinging to established routines as a life raft to prove the world hasn't ended -->
I get into bed. I lie entirely rigid on my side. I do not sleep. I watch the digital clock on Priya's microwave change numbers. 3:14. 4:22. 5:40. 

At 7:00 AM, the gray Ohio daylight starts creeping through the blinds. The humidity is already visible against the glass. 

I get up. I put on running shorts and a t-shirt. I go to my desk and open the container of oats. Half a cup of oats. Water. Microwave for two minutes. A giant spoonful of peanut butter stirred in. 

`` `It is Sunday morning. This is what I do on Sunday morning. I eat oatmeal and peanut butter. Therefore, everything is fine. The ecosystem is sustaining itself.` ``

I sit at my desk and eat it. It tastes like paste. I force myself to swallow it because it's the fastest thing that keeps me full, even though I have never felt emptier. 

My phone buzzes on the desk. 

*Mel: you alive? you never answered me about the arson.*

I stare at the text bubble. Mel is three hours away. If I were in her car right now, she would be talking, and I could just listen. I wouldn't have to formulate a version of the truth. 

I type back: *Alive. Fell asleep. Arson is always a valid methodology.*

I send it. Then I swipe over to my Notes app. 

<!-- Mental State: Analytical detachment; trying to convert trauma into observable data points to regain a sense of control -->
October 12, 2031
7:18 AM

I am eating my oatmeal. 
If you don't tell anyone about a variable, does it still affect the outcome of the grant?
When I was fourteen, Kayla told a boy my secret and it stopped being mine. 
If I tell Priya or Mel, it stops being mine. I become the Girl Who This Happened To. I become the punchline. 
I can't go to environmental ethics on Tuesday. 
But if I don't go, he knows why. 
He knows I am a thing he broke. 
I am going to go for a run tonight. I will listen to the same playlist. I will put my hair up. 
Nothing is fundamentally different about the chickadee today than yesterday. 
It just has to survive the winter. 

[DONE]
Continue

**My Analysis of "Continue" Prompt**

Okay, here’s the breakdown. This "Continue" prompt picks up after a deeply difficult moment. I need to move Dani Reyes forward, keeping her grounded and true to character.

<!-- Mental State: Planning/Strategic -->
First, I see the prompt. I have to progress through Sunday evening, with a run, and dread the upcoming Tuesday ethics class. I’ll make the run an attempt to find some kind of release, a physical exhaustion that mirrors her mental state.

I need to create narrative arc. It is a time jump to move us from the oatmeal morning to the class that will bring about emotional complications. Let's make this feel real and earned.

I’ll establish scenes: the run (body as a polluted space), a phone call/text that intrudes (Mom’s voice), and then Tuesday’s ethics class (confrontation and forced normalcy). I'll have Dani’s internal experience.

Her character is key here. It's the practical, observant side of her. Avoid being a "punchline/victim", use dad's bird/nature facts as grounding. Use oatmeal/routine as grounding, but, I will try to use the run as a counter-routine. Keep the writing tight. Short sentences, a slight detachment, the statistics-driven tone of an environmental science student. Hair tie snap, cheek biting, Lake Erie blooms, retaining walls, all can subtly work here.

Now, scene by scene:

*   **Scene 1:** Sunday evening. It's all about physical exhaustion as a distraction from the mental loops. The music, a playlist of defiance, the heavy body. This will be the Ohio water table. Polluted but not talked about.
*   **Scene 2:** Mom. A phone call during a break. Dani gives “a version.” The gulf between the truth and the surface is important.
*   **Scene 3:** Tuesday. Facing the space where he might be. Hyper-vigilance, an armor against intrusion. Mark walks in. Physiological response (unacknowledged).
*   **Scene 4:** The Notes app. Capturing the observations.

<!-- Mental State: Execution -->
I'm ready to write this. It's about being true to Dani and to the situation, pushing the tension, and making sure the emotional beats are right. No easy answers, just the raw reality.

I have to execute this precisely with HTML comments and double backticks for mental state and inner thoughts. I also need to close with `[DONE]`.
<!-- Mental State: Focused -->
Sunday evening. 6:30 PM. I lace up my running shoes. No plan.

<!-- Mental State: Determination/Discomfort -->
The boygenius song transitions to black midi. Then Paramore. I usually skip this song if anyone is around. But I let it play. `My legs feel heavy.` I bite my cheek. It's a physical reminder. `This body feels polluted, like the Ohio water table. No one's talking about it.`

<!-- Mental State: Vulnerability -->
Back in the dorm. Sweaty. Phone rings. Mom.

<!-- Mental State: Deflection/Fatigue -->
"Hey, Dani. How was your weekend?"
"Studied. Ran. Pretty quiet."
Mom is tired. She's on a break at the hospital. Another patient story. I listen. I give a version. `The gap is always there. The version and the truth.`

<!-- Mental State: Anticipation/Dread -->
Tuesday. 9:45 AM. Environmental Ethics.

<!-- Mental State: Hyper-Vigilance -->
I sit in the back. I don’t usually sit in the back.

<!-- Mental State: Fight or Flight -->
He walks in. Mark. Flannel shirt again. He doesn't look at me. `Does he look at me?` If he looks, he smiles. Like everything is normal. That's worse. The "joke/punchline" feeling.

<!-- Mental State: Processing/Observation -->
*Notes App*
Tuesday, 9:55 AM
Mark.

Sitting two rows ahead. Flannel.

I am not a joke. He doesn't get to do that.

My body.
Statistics.
One in *x*.

[DONE]

<!-- Mental State: Desperate for physical exhaustion to override the cognitive loop; attempting to reclaim her physical boundaries through exertion -->
Sunday, 6:30 PM. The sun is setting behind the brutalist architecture of the science buildings, turning the humid sky a bruised purple. I lace up my running shoes. I pull a sweatshirt on over my t-shirt even though it's too warm for it. I don't want any skin showing. 

I step out of the dorm and start running. No plan. Just a direction. 

My playlist is on shuffle. It starts with boygenius. The slow, melancholic harmonies make my chest feel impossibly tight, like I’m breathing through a straw. I pick up my pace, striking the pavement hard with my heels. 

`` `I am in control of my body. My muscles are contracting because I am telling them to. My lungs are burning because I am forcing them to work. I am the active variable.` ``

I run past the Thai place. Through the glass, I can see people sitting in the booths. I think about my pad see ew, medium spice, no egg. I wonder if the people inside are safe. I wonder how many of them are lying to the person across the table. 

The playlist jumps to black midi. Chaotic, math-rock noise. It matches the static in my brain. 

My legs feel heavier than usual. It’s not a cardiovascular failure; it’s a gravity problem. I am dragging an invisible weight. I run until I taste copper, biting the inside of my cheek without realizing it. I snap the hair tie on my right wrist. Snap. Snap.

<!-- Mental State: Compartmentalization; performing the "good daughter" routine to maintain the illusion of a baseline reality -->
When I get back to the dorm, the sweat is cold on my spine. Priya is at the library. My phone rings on my desk. It’s my mom. 

I stare at the caller ID. 

`` `If she hears my voice, she'll know. Moms are supposed to know. Her nursing instincts will kick in and she'll triage me over the phone.` ``

I clear my throat twice. I answer. "Hey, Mom."

"Hi, honey," she says. There's background noise—the squeak of rubber shoes on hospital floors, the distant beep of a monitor. "Just starting my shift. How was your weekend?"

"It was fine," I say. My voice is steady. A perfectly constructed retaining wall. "Pretty quiet. Just studying."

"Did you go to that party with Priya? The fraternity one?"

My stomach drops into absolute freefall. 

"Yeah, for a little bit," I say, giving her the version. "It was too crowded, though. You know me and crowds. I came back early."

"Good," she says, sounding distracted. "Frat parties are Petri dishes anyway. Your dad went out to the Metro Parks today. Saw a pileated woodpecker. He took a blurry photo of a tree and claims the bird is in it."

`` `Dad is looking at birds. Mom is doing her rounds. The world is exactly the same as it was on Friday. I am the only thing that has been altered.` ``

"Tell him I want to see the blurry tree," I say. I force a small laugh. 

"I will. Love you, Dani. Eat some vegetables."

"Love you too."

I hang up. She didn't know. I am a better liar than I thought, or she is worse at reading me. I'm not sure which is more depressing.

<!-- Mental State: Hyper-aroused flight-or-fight response masked by forced stillness; academic detachment used as a psychological shield -->
Tuesday, 9:45 AM. Environmental Ethics. 

I am sitting in the back row. I never sit in the back row. I am usually a middle-row person—engaged enough to show I care, far enough back to avoid being called on first. Today, I need my back against cinderblock. 

I have a heavy oversized sweater on. My hands are tucked deep into the sleeves. 

At 9:52, Mark walks in. 

He is wearing a grey Whitmore hoodie and carrying an iced coffee. He looks completely normal. His skin hasn't turned green. He doesn't have a scarlet letter burned into his chest. He looks like a guy who is going to get a B in Ethics and go on to work for a mid-tier environmental consulting firm. 

`` `How is he just walking? How is he drinking coffee? He poisoned the water table and he's just walking around drinking coffee.` ``

He scans the room. For a second, his eyes land on me in the back row. 

My breath stops. My short fingers curl into fists inside my sleeves. I wait for the acknowledgment. A grimace. A look of guilt. Anything. 

He gives me a tiny, polite smile—the kind you give a lab partner you mildly like—and turns around to sit two rows ahead of me. 

`` `He thinks it was a hookup. He actually thinks it was just a bad hookup. He didn't hear the "no." Or he heard it and decided "no" is just a sound women make sometimes. A punchline.` ``

The professor starts talking about the Clean Water Act of 1972. I open my laptop. I open the Notes app. 

<!-- Mental State: Bleak clarity; finalizing the internal narrative that this cannot be shared, locking the trauma away to preserve her dignity -->
October 14, 2031
10:15 AM

It is mathematically impossible for him to have not heard me. 
I am in the same room as him and the air is not catching fire. 
He smiled at me. 
If I tell the school, I have to explain why I went upstairs.
I have to explain why I agreed to the balcony. 
They will look at the data and say I was a willing participant in the early variables.
They will say the margin of error is too wide. 
Kayla made me a joke when I was fourteen. If I say anything now, Mark gets to tell his version. His version is that we went upstairs and I changed my mind too late. 
His version makes me the crazy girl who regrets a party. 
I am not going to be his punchline. 
I am just going to write grants. I am just going to do the statistics. 
I am going to keep eating my oatmeal. 
I will survive the winter.

[DONE]
Continue

**My Narrative Framework: Dani Reyes, Halloween, and the Price of Silence**

Okay, so I've got this Dani Reyes character – and the task is to keep her story moving after that initial class.  The brief is clear: maintain the format, keep the voice consistent, and move the narrative forward.  I need to show the deepening impact of the trauma on Dani, not just *tell* it. My plan hinges on highlighting the insidious nature of her coping mechanisms.

First, I need to pick up the narrative thread. It's Tuesday, post-class. We jump forward in time. Let's make it late October, Halloween weekend – October 31st, 2031, if we're being precise. The passage of time is crucial. The initial shock of the event has passed, and that physical reality of trauma, the aftermath, is setting in. The routine is becoming a cage.

I need to focus on her relationships, or rather, the *lack* of them.  Mel, her best friend, is a crucial anchor. I'll contrast Mel, who is texting her constantly but not calling, and Priya, her roommate, who is physically close, but at a distance. Mel is the emotional lifeline, the *what if* and Priya is the *now*.  The key will be showcasing that breaking point in the routine, so the oatmeal, the run, the Thai place – they all need to become charged, almost threatening. The Friday night outing will be a key pressure point.

I've sketched a Friday night scene. Let’s do it:

*   **Scene 1: Thai Place/Routine.** The same order. Pad see ew, medium spice, no egg. It's the physical act of repetition and then a slow burn that is the beginning of the erosion of her mental state. I'll use those science/nature metaphors here, like a 'closed loop' to demonstrate how she feels as though she is her own experiment.
*   **Scene 2: Dorm.** Halloween party prep with Priya. Priya wants Dani to go. Dani says no. Excuses.  I'll emphasize Priya's concern. This is the moment she says "I miss you."
*   **Scene 3: Mel's Voice Memo.** The voice memo will be trivial, a touchstone to a shared past. The contrast between that memory of intimacy and Dani's current isolation will be heartbreaking.
*   **Scene 4: The Notes App.** Dani's internal monologue and reflections.

The voice needs to be consistent. I’ll keep that science/nature imagery.  Dad's hands, biting her cheek, the hair tie – these are tangible anchors.  I need to show the mental state, not just tell it.

*   I'll start with this initial hyper-fixation on routine. `Hyper-fixation on routine; using predictability as a trauma response to avoid triggers.`
*   Halloween night, I'll pick up the food, and then back to the dorm. I will show Priya trying to reach her. I want that sense of being "warm" but distant. Then there is an exhaustion that becomes clear. `Exhaustion from masking; the creeping realization that isolation is just another form of being trapped.`
*   The hair tie snapping will symbolize that breaking point. `She doesn't know she's talking to a ghost.`
*   Finally, the Notes app: the core of her feelings. It will show the truth is not a release, but calcifying inside her.

As I draft, I will be vigilant about these elements:

*   Does it *feel* like Dani? Yes, the birds and the statistical language are there.
*   Are the HTML comments, backticks, and Notes app formatting consistent? Yes.
*   Is the narrative arc clear? I will re-check the dates.
*   And importantly, does the text highlight the *physical* manifestation of her anxiety? The hair tie, the food, the missed connection of Mel.

[DONE]

<!-- Mental State: Rigid reliance on established patterns; treating routine as a sterile environment where anomalies cannot occur -->
Friday, October 31. The air has finally lost its humidity, turning sharp and brittle. The leaves on the oaks outside the science building are brown and curling. 

I am standing at the counter of the Thai place near campus. 

"Pad see ew, medium spice, no egg," the guy behind the counter says before I even open my mouth. He knows me. He taps it into the iPad. 

"Yeah. Thanks."

`` `I am a closed loop. No new variables. Predictable inputs yield predictable outputs. If I never deviate, I never have to calculate for disaster.` ``

I take the heavy plastic bag and walk the four blocks back to the dorm. I keep my eyes on the concrete. I don't look at the groups of students heading out in the early dark, shivering in their costumes. 

When I open the door to my room, Priya is applying silver glitter to her eyelids. She is wearing a metallic dress. She looks incredible, bright and alive and completely unburdened by the physics of other people.
